text.title: Project Closure Core Rules
text.summary: 6 testable rules grounding the project-closure methodology. Each rule pairs a binary check with a sourced rationale.

rule.statement: Obtain written sign-off from the accepting authority for every deliverable before declaring closure complete. Silence does not equal acceptance.
rule.rationale: Ambiguous completion creates re-opened projects, warranty disputes, and resource limbo. A signed artefact is the only auditable evidence.

rule.statement: Acceptance criteria must be observable and agreed before closure starts — they cannot be written retrospectively to match delivered output.
rule.rationale: Retrospective criteria always pass; they destroy the value of the gate.

rule.statement: Conduct lessons learned before the team disperses; schedule it at project kickoff as a fixed deliverable.
rule.rationale: Memories fade within days and teams scatter to new projects within hours of delivery; a session two weeks post-close captures platitudes, not facts.

rule.statement: Decommission infrastructure in stages: read-only first, then revoke writes, then deprovision. Default to 30-day soft-delete before hard deletion.
rule.rationale: Staged decommission allows recovery if handover is incomplete; hard-deletion of cloud resources risks irreversible data loss.

rule.statement: Sequence access revocation after operations handover acknowledgement — never revoke before the receiving team confirms readiness.
rule.rationale: Premature revocation breaks ongoing operations and is harder to reverse than to delay.

rule.statement: For cancelled projects, document the cancellation rationale in a signed document with the sponsor's name.
rule.rationale: Cancelled project rationale is the most frequently lost institutional knowledge; same idea resurfaces 18 months later with the same failure mode.

list.item: Project still has open scope, unresolved issues, or unsigned acceptance — resolve those first
Operational or run-rate work with no defined endpoint — use service transition documents instead
Long-tail support work after handover — that is operations, not project closure
